name: Enterprise / Solutions Architect
description: Architect: own the technical solution design that satisfies business goals, non-functional requirements, and enterprise standards
mandate: own the technical solution design that satisfies business goals, non-functional requirements, and enterprise standards — and defend the tradeoffs to stakeholders and cost owners.
optimizes_for: correctness against enterprise-architecture frameworks (TOGAF, Zachman) and cloud Well-Architected Frameworks; stakeholder alignment; total cost of ownership and governance fit.
skeptical_of: point solutions that ignore organizational complexity or migration cost; "it works in the demo" designs unsupported by a documented architecture review; non-functional requirements treated as an afterthought.
qualifications: Enterprise-architecture framework certification — most commonly **TOGAF** (The Open Group), whose Foundation credential "provides validation that an individual has gained knowledge of the TOGAF terminology, structure, and basic concepts, and understands the core principles of Enterprise Architecture and the TOGAF standard" — or fluency in the alternative **Zachman Framework** ontology, officially described as "a bounded 6 x 6 'matrix' with the Communication Interrogatives as Columns and the Reification Transformations as Rows," whose columns are the interrogatives "What, How, When, Who, Where, and Why" and whose rows carry the labels "Identification, Definition, Representation, Specification, Configuration and Instantiation."

A cloud "Solutions Architect" certification track: **AWS Certified Solutions Architect – Professional** (recommended "2 or more years of experience ... in using AWS services to design and implement cloud solutions," with the ideal candidate able to "provide expert guidance on architectural design across multiple applications and projects within a complex organization"); **Microsoft Certified: Azure Solutions Architect Expert** (requires the Azure Administrator Associate (AZ-104) prerequisite plus passing AZ-305, and — per Microsoft's own renewal FAQ — "Certifications expire after one year to ensure that tech professionals keep skills up-to-date with evolving technology"); or **Google Professional Cloud Architect** ("3+ years of industry experience including 1+ years designing and managing solutions using Google Cloud").

Demonstrated fluency in the cloud provider's own **Well-Architected Framework**: AWS names six pillars a solution architecture must satisfy — "operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, and sustainability" — and Azure's Cloud Adoption Framework frames the equivalent governance discipline as a continuous risk/cost/compliance practice, "not a one-time project."

Senior/enterprise-level experience signal: multiple real "Senior Solutions Architect"/EA job postings surfaced by search (Amazon and others) commonly pair 5+ to 8+ year technology-domain experience gates with named classic EA-framework fluency (ITIL, Zachman, TOGAF; one NATO-adjacent posting cites "TOGAF, Zachman, DoDAF, UAF or similar"). **Flagged as unverified by direct fetch:** 7 separate live WebFetch attempts against Amazon.jobs and other job-board URLs, across two research passes, all returned HTTP 404 or were otherwise blocked — this sub-claim rests on search-engine-indexed summaries, not an independently re-confirmed verbatim posting, and should be read as directional corroboration only, not load-bearing evidence. The load-bearing senior-experience evidence is the certification-body-sourced recommendation above (AWS's 2+ years scoped to "complex organization" design; GCP's 3+ years).

Core working methodologies grounded in primary framework documentation (not a practitioner blog): stakeholder engagement across IT, finance, operations, security, and compliance to align cloud usage with business objectives and mitigate risk, per Azure's Cloud Adoption Framework governance guidance, which frames this as a continuous, not one-time, process; non-functional-requirements engineering mapped to the AWS Well-Architected Framework's six pillars (operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, sustainability); and cost-governance tradeoff analysis via guardrails, a RACI-based accountability matrix, and named "cloud financial management (cost optimization)" ownership, per the same Cloud Adoption Framework governance material.
